notes: Critical host plant for monarch butterflies. Tap-rooted, dislikes transplanting once established.
Long-blooming prairie staple. Leave seedheads for goldfinches in winter.
Often grown as a short-lived perennial; self-seeds readily.
Mint-family aromatic. Powdery mildew possible in humid sites.
Tall purple spikes bloom top-down. Great for cut flowers.
Early-spring nectar for returning ruby-throated hummingbirds.
Streamside and rain-garden favorite. Short-lived but self-seeds.
Tough, drought-tolerant. Deadhead for repeat bloom.
Late-season pollinator powerhouse. Does NOT cause hay fever.
Key fall nectar source for monarch migration.
Long-lived, shrub-like with a deep taproot. Nitrogen fixer.
Evergreen basal rosette in winter. Reddish-purple foliage on 'Husker Red'.
Tall back-of-border with vanilla-scented flowers.
Stunning saturated purple. Pair with late goldenrods.
Fragrant spring woodland groundcover.
Spreads gently by stolons. Lovely shade groundcover.
Marbled basal foliage; airy flower panicles.
Arching stems with pendant flowers; blue-black berries.
Heart-shaped leaves form dense groundcover; ground-level flowers.
Spring ephemeral with brilliant white flowers and red sap.
Brilliant fall color. Tolerates wet feet.
Iconic understory tree. Watch for anthracnose; site with good airflow.
Pea-family flowers cover bare branches in early spring.
Keystone species supporting hundreds of caterpillar species.
Sinewy gray bark; tolerates shade and seasonal flooding.
Early white blooms, edible June berries, vivid fall color.
Fragrant late-fall blooms after the leaves drop.
Host plant for spicebush swallowtail. Aromatic foliage.
Fragrant cascading racemes; brilliant crimson fall color.
Large shrub/small tree. Edible blue-black drupes loved by birds.
Iconic prairie bunchgrass with copper-red fall and winter color.
Tallgrass-prairie dominant. Three-pronged seedhead resembles turkey foot.
Golden plumes in fall; warm-season tallgrass.
Airy seed panicles. Versatile across moisture conditions.
Oat-like seeds hang on one side of stem. Drought-loving.
Fine-textured fountain of foliage; flowers smell like coriander.
Lawn-alternative for dry shade. Spreads by short rhizomes.
Evergreen leathery fronds; tolerates dry shade once established.
Edible fiddleheads in spring. Spreads aggressively by rhizomes.
Delicate lacy fronds; clump-forming, well-behaved.
Evergreen, leathery; reliable in dry shade.
Cinnamon-colored fertile fronds rise from center of vase.
Black wiry stems with delicate fan-shaped pinnae.
Vigorous climbing vine; brilliant scarlet fall color.
Native alternative to invasive Japanese honeysuckle.
Frothy late-summer flowers followed by feathery seedheads.
Host for Gulf fritillary. Spectacular intricate flowers.
Woodland edge classic; deeply lobed foliage.
Spring ephemeral; pink buds open to sky-blue bells, then dormant by summer.
Ohio's state wildflower. Source only nursery-propagated; slow to establish.
Spring ephemeral; flowers resemble upside-down pantaloons.
Mottled foliage like trout markings; ephemeral spring colony.
Tiny pink-veined flowers carpet woodlands in early spring.
Wetland margins and rain gardens. Toxic to humans/pets if ingested.
Fused leaves form cups that hold rainwater for birds.
Host for black swallowtail. Yellow umbels in late spring.
Host plant for Baltimore checkerspot butterfly.
Monarch host. Loves wet feet but adapts to garden soil.
Most familiar monarch host. Spreads aggressively by rhizomes.
Pierced leaves; flat-topped white flowers; pollinator magnet.
Drooping pink umbels on slender stems; edible.
Top-tier pollinator plant; minty fragrance when crushed.
Tall candelabra of slender flower spikes.
Misleading name — doesn't cause sneezing. Late-season color.
Evergreen rosette spreads to form a deer-resistant groundcover.
Umbrella-like colonies in spring. All parts toxic except ripe fruit.

commonNames: Feathery False Lily of the Valley
notes: Plumes of tiny flowers at branch tips; red berries in fall.
Tall slender spires of white above dark foliage.
Spreads aggressively — best in naturalized areas or rain gardens.
Spherical pincushion flowers; thrives at pond edges.
Edible cooked berries used for syrup and wine.
Glossy black berries packed with antioxidants; brilliant fall color.
Dioecious; need male nearby for berries. Brilliant red winter fruit.
Peeling bark gives the name; tough and adaptable.
Excellent erosion-controlling shrub; striking red fall color.
Tallest eastern hardwood. Tulip-shaped greenish-yellow flowers.
Best-in-class scarlet fall color. Key nectar source for honey.
Exfoliating salmon-cream bark. Tolerates wet sites; resists bronze birch borer.
Massive shade tree with white mottled bark; floodplain native.
Largest edible fruit native to North America. Host for zebra swallowtail.
Mitten-shaped leaves; spectacular orange-red fall color. Host for spicebush swallowtail.
Fast-growing oak with brilliant red fall color. Acorns mature in two seasons; supports hundreds of moth and butterfly larvae.
Massive, drought- and fire-tolerant prairie oak. Largest acorns of any North American oak; thick corky bark.
Upland oak with deeply furrowed dark bark and orange inner bark. Tolerates dry, sandy soils.
Distinctive chestnut-like leaves with coarse teeth. Prefers limestone soils; sweet acorns favored by wildlife.
Pyramidal shape with classic deeply-lobed leaves and persistent lower branches. Tolerates wet, poorly drained sites.
Unmistakable shaggy gray bark in long curling strips. Edible sweet nuts; golden fall color.
Upland hickory of dry oak-hickory forests. Bitter nuts but excellent wildlife value; golden fall color.
Distinctive bright yellow winter buds. Tolerates wetter sites than most hickories; nuts too bitter for human use.
The classic maple-syrup tree; iconic orange-red fall color. Long-lived but salt- and pollution-sensitive.
Smooth gray bark and copper-bronze marcescent winter leaves. Edible nuts; shade-tolerant climax forest species.
Valuable hardwood timber; produces juglone, allelopathic to many garden plants. Edible nuts; aromatic foliage.
Fragrant summer flowers are a major nectar source for bees. Heart-shaped leaves; soft straight-grained wood.
Pendant racemes of white flowers, then dark cherries devoured by birds. Host plant for over 400 lepidoptera species.
Sweet edible fruit ripens after frost; dioecious so both sexes needed for fruit. Distinctive blocky bark.
Graceful evergreen of cool ravines and north slopes. Threatened by hemlock woolly adelgid; needs consistent moisture.
Soft blue-green needles in bundles of five. Fast-growing, long-lived; salt- and pollution-sensitive.
Tough pioneer evergreen with blue berry-like cones loved by waxwings. Aromatic wood; tolerates poor dry soils.
Only magnolia native to the Midwest. Tulip-shaped greenish-yellow flowers, cucumber-like fruits ripening to red.
Flat white flower clusters followed by blue-black berries; reliable rich-red fall color. Tough and adaptable.
Understory shrub for dry shade where little else thrives. Pink-to-purple fall foliage; black drupes.
Brilliant red winter stems for landscape interest. Suckers to form thickets; excellent for rain gardens.
Wetland dogwood with porcelain-blue fruit. Reddish-purple young stems; suckers to form dense thickets.
Flat creamy-white flower clusters; the native parent of cultivars like Annabelle. Blooms on new wood — prune late winter.
Frothy white midsummer flowers; nitrogen-fixing. Leaves were brewed as a tea substitute during the Revolutionary War.
Edible nuts in fringed husks; among the earliest spring catkins. Suckers into thickets — useful hedgerow plant.
Edible summer berries and superb scarlet fall color. Needs acidic soil (pH 4.5–5.5); plant multiple cultivars for cross-pollination.
Tough, adaptable native shrub honeysuckle (unlike the invasive species). Excellent for slopes; red-tinged fall foliage.
Pyramidal panicles of white flowers in mid-summer. Good for wet meadows and pond edges.
Low-growing native rose with single pink flowers and red hips. Far better-behaved than invasive multiflora rose.
Trumpet-shaped flowers in spring are a hummingbird magnet. Semi-evergreen with bronzy winter color; well-behaved climber.
Vigorous summer-blooming hummingbird vine; can spread aggressively by suckers. Give it strong support and room to roam.
Huge heart-shaped leaves form dense screens. Curious pipe-shaped flowers hidden in foliage; host plant for pipevine swallowtail.
Native alternative to invasive Asian wisterias — shorter racemes but far less aggressive. Blooms on new wood.
Edible starchy tubers — a staple food of Indigenous peoples. Fragrant chocolate-purple flowers; nitrogen-fixing.
Named for Ohio's Muskingum River. Whorls of arching foliage resemble miniature palm fronds; tolerates flooding.
Forms iconic raised tussocks in wet meadows and sedge swamps. Foundation species for many wetland communities.
Distinctive spiky star-shaped seed heads excellent in dried arrangements. Tolerates seasonal flooding.
Cool-season bunchgrass; one of the few natives that grows well in part shade. Quick establishment for restorations.
Airy bottlebrush-shaped seed heads in dappled woodland shade. Cool-season; one of the best native grasses for shade.
Dangling oat-like seedheads turn bronze in fall. Self-seeds enthusiastically; excellent for shaded slopes.
Clumping rush with rounded green stems and tan flower clusters near the tip. Foundation plant for rain gardens and pond margins.
Named for sensitivity to first frost. Distinctive bead-like fertile fronds persist through winter; spreads by rhizomes.
Small evergreen fern with glossy dark-purple stems. Tolerates dry shade and rocky sites including limestone outcrops.
Small evergreen fern that colonizes mossy boulders and rock crevices. Curls when dry, rehydrates after rain.
Among the earliest spring ephemerals. Three-lobed leaves persist through winter; flowers in white, pink, lavender, or blue.
Named for Thomas Jefferson. Distinctive butterfly-shaped paired leaves; fleeting white flowers in early spring.
Lacy blue-green foliage emerges purple-tinged. Striking persistent dark-blue berries; all parts toxic if ingested.
Drooping pale-yellow bell flowers on arching stems. Perfoliate leaves (stem appears to pierce the leaf); slow to establish.
Ladder-like pinnate leaves and clusters of sky-blue bell flowers in spring. Good clump-former for woodland gardens.
Graceful arching stems with axillary flower clusters along their length. One of the best goldenrods for woodland edges.
Drought-tolerant white-flowered aster for dry shade. Heart-shaped basal leaves; airy late-season blooms.
Smooth blue-green foliage and a long display of light-blue daisies in fall. Drought tolerant once established.
Heart-shaped basal leaves and clouds of small lavender-blue flowers. Excellent late-season pollinator plant for shade.
Spikes of vivid blue tubular flowers in late summer. Pair with Cardinal Flower (Lobelia cardinalis) for a dramatic blue-and-red display.
Snapdragon-like lavender flowers on square stems through summer. Loves consistently wet feet — perfect for rain gardens.
Showy yellow flower clusters and tropical-looking pinnate foliage. Host plant for sulphur butterflies; nitrogen-fixing.
Massive astilbe-like white flower plumes on shrub-sized clumps. Dioecious — males have showier, longer-lasting plumes.
Towering coreopsis with anise-scented foliage. Sturdy stems rarely need staking despite the height; long bloom period.
Ohio's state tree. All parts are toxic to humans and livestock; the polished brown nuts are traditionally carried as good-luck charms. Among the first trees to leaf out in spring.
Fast-growing floodplain tree with deeply lobed leaves silvery on the underside. Brittle wood drops branches in storms — site away from structures.
The only dogwood with alternate leaves — all others are opposite. Distinctive tiered horizontal branches give it the common name. Bluish-black drupes ripen on red stems.
Clusters of persistent coral-pink berries hold through winter, well after the leaves drop. Suckers freely — useful for stabilizing slopes and banks.
Distinctive three-chambered inflated seed pods rattle in the wind. Understory shrub of moist deciduous woods; suckers into thickets over time.
Resembles a true sunflower but blooms longer and stays a manageable size. Tolerates clay and drought once established.
Drooping yellow petals around a tall gray-brown cone. Crushed cones smell of anise. A signature prairie species.
Smaller flowers than black-eyed Susan but borne in massive airy sprays late into autumn. Reliably self-seeds; treat as a short-lived perennial colony.
Wet-meadow giant with frothy cotton-candy plumes 5–7 ft tall. Needs consistent moisture; wilts dramatically in drought.
Flowers never fully open. Bumblebees are nearly the only pollinators strong enough to pry the petals apart, making this a specialist resource late in the season.
Fragrant pyramidal flower clusters carry the late-summer border. Prone to powdery mildew — site with morning sun and good airflow.
Enormous sandpaper-textured spade-shaped basal leaves anchor a nearly leafless flower stalk that towers 6–10 ft. Deep taproot makes it drought-proof but resentful of transplanting.
Spring ephemeral bulb of moist woods and prairies. Pale blue spires above grass-like foliage; goes fully dormant by midsummer.
The native bittersweet — not the invasive Oriental C. orbiculatus that displaces it. Yellow capsules split to reveal orange-red arils; female plants only fruit if a male is nearby.
Smothered in tiny white daisies on bushy, needle-leaved stems in fall. Tolerates poor dry soils where showier asters fail.
Largest of the native buckeyes, with the cleanest summer foliage and best orange-red fall color. Smooth nuts lack the spines of Ohio buckeye. All parts toxic.
Distinctive four-angled twigs and inner bark that yields a blue dye in water. Notably more resistant to emerald ash borer than green or white ash, making it a hopeful native option.
Best red-fall-color oak — deep scarlet foliage persists into late autumn. Thrives in dry, sandy upland soils where many oaks struggle.
Fast-growing red oak group member with brilliant russet-red fall color. Adaptable to urban conditions including clay and drought once established.

Historically the quintessential American street tree, with vase-shaped silhouette. Dutch elm disease devastated wild populations; modern DED-resistant cultivars ('Princeton', 'Valley Forge', 'Jefferson') restore the form.
Dense, drooping white flower racemes followed by dark purple cherries beloved by birds. Foliage and pits cyanogenic — toxic to livestock. Suckers to form thickets.

Velvety antler-fuzzed branches and crimson seed-cone "candles" that persist through winter. Brilliant scarlet-orange fall color. Spreads aggressively by root suckers — plant where colony formation is welcome.
The commercial wild blueberry of Maine — sweet small berries on a low spreading groundcover. Requires acidic, well-drained soil. Brilliant scarlet fall foliage.

Iconic Appalachian evergreen with intricate cup-shaped pink flowers — anthers spring-loaded under tension, snapping forward when triggered by visiting bees. Needs acidic, well-drained soil. All parts toxic.
Ohio's most common evergreen wood fern. Lacy, finely-cut fronds remain green through winter — anchor of the cold-season shade garden.

Distinctive habit: fertile spore-bearing pinnae appear mid-frond, "interrupting" the leafy green stretches above and below. Statuesque vase shape; one of the oldest fern lineages — fossil record back 200 million years.

Cherished spring edible with garlic-onion flavor. Smooth lily-like leaves emerge in early spring and die back before the white umbel flowers bloom in summer. Conservation concern from commercial overharvest — sustainable harvest takes only leaves, leaving bulbs intact.

Slender thread-like leaves in whorls give a delicate haze quite unlike other milkweeds. Monarch host plant adapted to dry, lean soils where common milkweed declines. Late bloomer for the milkweed sequence.
Tallest of the Joe-pye weeds, with a distinctively hollow stem and large rounded dome-shaped flower clusters. Magnet for swallowtails and monarchs. Formerly *Eupatorium fistulosum*.
Most garden-compatible Joe-pye — shorter and stiffer than hollow-stem, with purple-spotted stems and flatter flower clusters. Formerly *Eupatorium maculatum*.
Tall late-summer sunflower with edible nutty tubers — a major Indigenous food crop predating European contact. Spreads aggressively by tubers; contain it in a defined bed or root barrier.
Diminutive 6-inch woodland iris with pale blue flowers marked by yellow-and-white crests. Spreads by stoloniferous rhizomes into elegant colonies. Beloved spring shade-garden plant.
Brilliant nodding orange Turk's-cap flowers with strongly reflexed petals. Wet meadow and prairie lily; the Midwest counterpart to Turk's cap lily.
Showstopper native lily — single plants can bear up to 40 nodding orange flowers in a candelabra. Distinguishing green star at the throat. Tallest of native lilies.
Premier evergreen woodland groundcover phlox — spreads by surface stolons into dense mats. Sheets of fragrant lilac-blue flowers in mid-spring. Distinct from rock-garden moss phlox (*P. subulata*).

Penn State and Mt. Cuba pollinator trials repeatedly ranked this #1 for insect diversity and abundance. Cloud of tiny white flowers over fine needle-like foliage all summer. Spreads by rhizomes — give it room.
Parent species of the ubiquitous 'Goldsturm' cultivar — true perennial unlike the biennial *R. hirta*. Daisy-flowered for months; seed heads feed goldfinches into winter.
Compact, well-behaved goldenrod for dry sites where taller species flop. Gray-green foliage; arched one-sided flower plumes. Excellent for poor sandy soils.
Most ornamental of the goldenrods — upright pyramidal flower plumes with reddish-purple stems. Clump-forming rather than aggressively spreading. Outstanding for sunny borders.

Bright yellow 2-inch poppy flowers above lobed blue-green foliage. Self-sows freely into delightful colonies. Often confused with invasive greater celandine (*Chelidonium majus*); native has hairy fuzzy seedpods, not smooth.
Clear sky-blue daisy flowers in airy panicles on wiry stems. Drought-tolerant prairie aster named after Ohio's Olentangy River, where it was first described. Formerly *Aster oolentangiensis*.

Powder-blue three-petaled flowers open in morning and close by afternoon, replaced daily for weeks. Arching grass-like foliage. State flower of nothing — but well-loved in Ohio gardens. Cut back hard mid-summer for a fresh second flush.

Iconic deep maroon-red spring ephemeral — three petals, three sepals, three leaves. Fly-pollinated, with a faint carrion scent (hence "Stinking Benjamin"). Slow-growing from seed; never dig from the wild.

Most common native cool-season prairie grass. Distinctive arching nodding seed heads with long curved awns — wheat-like but more graceful. Quick to establish; useful as a 'nurse grass' in prairie plantings.

Late-summer reddish-purple flower panicles form a glowing haze above the foliage, often as wide as the plant is tall. Mature panicles break off and tumbleweed in autumn winds. Thrives in poor sandy soil.

Iconic 6-foot wet-prairie grass with arching glossy leaves edged with tiny silica teeth (sharp — wear gloves). Spreads aggressively by rhizomes; brilliant gold fall color. Excellent for stabilizing pond margins and rain gardens.
Bears the largest nuts of any native hickory — sweet, edible kernels in thick husks. Floodplain tree with shaggy peeling bark like shagbark hickory but on wetter sites.

Extremely tough urban shade tree — handles compacted soil, drought, salt, pollution. Distinctive corky warty bark. Host plant for hackberry emperor and tawny emperor butterflies. Persistent purple drupes feed winter birds.

Coarse architectural silhouette and massive bipinnately compound leaves — among the last trees to leaf out, first to drop. Tolerates urban abuse. Seeds inside the leathery brown pods are toxic raw; settlers roasted them as a coffee substitute (don't try this).
Glossy five-pointed star-shaped leaves turn extraordinary mixed red-purple-orange-yellow in fall, often on a single tree. Spiny "gumball" fruits drop in quantity — a nuisance on lawns and walks.
Tropical-looking small tree with 2-foot leaves clustered at branch tips like umbrella spokes. Large white flowers smell musky-sweet. Rose-colored fruit cones in fall.

Edible blackberry-like fruit in early summer — sweet, juicy, beloved by birds. Threatened by hybridization with introduced white mulberry (*M. alba*). Distinguished by hairy leaf undersides and longer dull-tipped buds.
Small understory tree with distinctive papery hop-like seed clusters. Exceptionally hard wood (hence "ironwood"). Shaggy strip-peeling bark; slow-growing but very long-lived.

Drooping racemes of fragrant white lily-of-the-valley flowers in mid-summer — unusual bloom time for a tree. Brilliant deep-red fall color appears with the persistent seed pods. Source of the prized 'sourwood honey'.

One of the fastest-growing eastern trees — 6+ ft/year. Triangular leaves flutter audibly on flattened petioles. Female trees release cotton-like seeds in early summer (use male cultivars to avoid). Brittle wood drops branches; site away from structures.

Small ornamental understory tree with greenish-white flowers and unusual papery winged-disc fruits (the "wafers") in dense clusters. Host plant for giant swallowtail butterfly. Citrus-family aroma in crushed leaves.
Defining ridge-top oak of the eastern Appalachian uplands. Deeply furrowed dark blocky bark and chestnut-like wavy-edged leaves. Sweet acorns prized by wildlife. Formerly *Q. prinus*.

Largest native willow — a true tree, not shrub. Rough dark furrowed bark. Critical streambank stabilizer; host plant for viceroy and mourning cloak butterflies. Bark is the original source of salicylic acid (aspirin).

Medicinal mucilaginous inner bark — long used as a sore-throat lozenge. Distinguished from American elm by sandpaper-rough leaf surface and hairy buds. Less susceptible to Dutch elm disease but not immune.

Suckering thorny shrub-tree, host plant for giant swallowtail butterfly (the largest in OH). Chewing the bark numbs the mouth — traditional toothache remedy. Sister to the source of Sichuan peppercorns.

Pioneer pine of poor dry sites — eroded slopes, abandoned fields, strip-mine reclamation. Short twisted needles in pairs, persistent prickly cones. Once the dominant Christmas tree of the mid-Atlantic.

Aromatic flat-spray evergreen foliage; the species behind countless landscape arborvitae cultivars. Tolerates wet soils most evergreens won't. Heavily browsed by deer where populations are high — protect or site carefully.

Native low-spreading evergreen — a rare shade-tolerant native conifer groundcover. Bright-red berry-like arils ripen on female plants. Foliage and seeds toxic to humans/livestock; arils edible (the seed is not). Heavily deer-browsed.
Tough multi-stemmed thicket-forming shrub for hedges and naturalizing. White berries on red pedicels are gone by mid-fall — birds clean them off. Tolerates almost any soil; spreads by root suckers.

Among the earliest native shrubs to bloom — pale yellow flowers open before leaves emerge. Bark is famously flexible and rope-strong; impossible to snap a young branch. Slow-growing connoisseur shrub for woodlands.
Tidy mounded shrub smothered in bright yellow puffball flowers all summer — long bloom rare in shrubs. Thrives in poor dry rocky soils where many shrubs fail. Distinctive reddish exfoliating bark.

Distinctive winged "rachis" between leaflets along the central leaf stalk. Glossy compound leaves turn brilliant scarlet in fall — among the showiest fall-color shrubs. Drought-tolerant pioneer. Listed as *R. copallina* in older sources.
Sister to staghorn sumac but with smooth (not fuzzy) twigs. Crimson seed cones persist all winter — used to make 'sumac-ade'. Brilliant red fall color. Suckers freely into colonies.

Ohio's only native climbing rose. Trailing-arching canes can scramble 15 ft up a structure or sprawl as a thicket. Single five-petaled pink flowers fade to white; bright red hips in fall. Surprisingly disease-resistant.
Showy 2-inch rose-purple flowers all summer — one of the few thornless brambles. Maple-shaped leaves to 10 inches across. Dry shallow red raspberries are edible but bland. Suckers into colonies.

Pyramidal clusters of bright red berries ripen by midsummer — earlier than common elderberry, which has flat-topped purple clusters. Berries are eaten by birds but raw fruit/seeds are toxic to humans (cooked jams use them). Formerly *S. pubens*.
Drier-site sibling of *V. angustifolium*, tolerating rockier and poorer soils. Pale blue-green foliage gives the species name. Sweet small blueberries; brilliant red-purple fall color.

Taller bluebery relative with open spreading habit and distinctive flared (not urn-shaped) white bell flowers showing prominent stamens. Greenish-yellow fruits are sour — birds and deer eat them readily, but not great for humans.

Bright lime-green lacy fronds release a fresh-cut hay scent when bruised. Spreads aggressively by rhizomes into solid groundcover — overwhelms wildflowers if not contained. Excellent for difficult dry shade slopes.
Erect-arching fronds with linear sori that look silvery-white before maturing — hence the common name. Clump-forming, not aggressive. Easy and overlooked native for the shade garden.
Unusual narrow undivided pinnae give an unfern-like look — almost grass-like ladder. Striking architectural texture in a shade planting. Slowly colonizes by rhizomes.
Largest of the native wood ferns — broad arching fronds to 4 ft. Distinguished by dark scaly stipes and broad-based fronds that abruptly taper at the tip. Statement plant for rich shaded soils.
Distinctive broad triangular fronds held nearly horizontal. Slow spreader by rhizomes into open colonies, ideal for naturalistic woodlands. Native counterpart to the larger Goldie's fern look.

Diagnostic 'tapered at both ends' frond — narrows to a point at the tip AND at the base, like a tapered torpedo. Aggressive colonizer; smothers spring ephemerals where it takes hold. Best in defined areas.

Reliably self-sows from year to year — establishes a returning 'annual' colony. Sensitive leaflets fold when touched. Major food source for bobwhite quail and many bumblebees; nitrogen-fixer for poor soils.
Pale yellow cousin of the more common orange jewelweed. Hollow translucent stems; ripe seedpods explode at the slightest touch. Folk remedy for poison ivy and stinging nettle exposure.
Tall biennial spike of star-shaped sky-blue flowers (not the typical bellflower shape) — blooms in upper leaf axils all summer. Self-sows reliably on woodland edges.

Spring white flowers ripen into one of the strangest fruits in the woods: porcelain-white berries with a black 'pupil' on thick red stalks, lasting months. All parts highly toxic — never eat the berries.
Delicate spring ephemeral that spreads by thin rhizomes into wide carpets. Single white star flower above five-parted whorled leaves. Disappears by midsummer.
Small white-green flowers atop tall wiry stems mature into thimble-shaped seed heads that burst into cotton tufts — striking late-fall feature. Tolerates dry shade where many anemones won't.

Cousin of Jack-in-the-pulpit but stranger — a slender pale-green spadix extends 6 inches past the spathe, like a flicking tongue. Striking bright-red fruit cluster in late summer. Single horizontally-held leaf divided into 7-15 leaflets.
Restoration-favorite milkweed — looks like common milkweed but with smooth (not fuzzy) leaves and more refined garden habit. Monarch host plant. Clump-forming rather than aggressively spreading.
Tall elegant spires of white pea-flowers on dark stems. Nitrogen-fixer; shrub-like clump-forming habit that holds shape all season. Black seedpods rattle in fall — popular dried-arrangement element.
Most compact Baptisia — bushy 2-ft mound of small clover-like leaves with bright yellow pea-flowers across the top. Excellent for dry sandy soils. Foliage turns silvery-black with frost.
Tiered stack of small lavender bee-attracting flower heads, each ringed with showy pink-purple bracts. Aromatic mint-family foliage. Tolerates drier sites than its sibling *B. hirsuta*.
Shade-tolerant sister of *B. ciliata* — stacked tiers of dotted-lavender bee flowers above hairy aromatic foliage. Long bloom; reliably overlooked native for the woodland edge.
Common spring ephemeral with deeply cut whorled leaves and clusters of white-pink four-petaled flowers. Host plant for the West Virginia white butterfly. Disappears by early summer.
Lemon-scented foliage; airy panicles of small tubular yellow flowers with fringed lower lips. One of the few mints that thrives in deep shade. Stoneroot rhizome is a traditional herbal remedy.

Fluffy late-season blue-purple flat-topped flower heads — looks like a hardy ageratum. Major late-season nectar source for migrating monarchs. Spreads by rhizomes; give it room or a contained bed. Formerly *Eupatorium coelestinum*.
Late-season oregano-scented native mint. Tiny lavender flowers in axillary clusters. Late-fall novelty: stems exude ribbons of "frost flowers" — extruded ice ribbons — on the first freezes.

Close sibling of Dutchman's breeches — same lacy blue-green foliage, but heart-shaped (not pantaloon-shaped) fragrant white flowers. Named for the yellow corn-kernel-like underground bulblets. True spring ephemeral.
Tall wet-meadow aster with flat-topped umbrella clusters of small white daisies — distinctive among asters. Mt. Cuba aster trials showed high pollinator value. Formerly *Aster umbellatus*.
White-flowered sister of the more common yellow trout lily. Mottled olive-brown leaves emerge like trout flanks. Nodding reflexed-petal flower for a few weeks in early spring; foliage gone by June.
Airy 'baby's breath of the prairie' — cloud of tiny white flower-like bracts on wiry stems for months. Brilliant orange-red fall color. Milky sap can irritate skin (typical of Euphorbia).
Drier-site Joe-pye, with white flat-topped flower clusters instead of pink. Tolerates rocky upland soils where the wetland-loving siblings die. Formerly *Eupatorium altissimum*.

Classic evergreen woodland groundcover. Glossy oval leaves smell of wintergreen when crushed (source of the flavor before synthetics). Bright red berries persist all winter — minty, edible. Needs acidic well-drained soil.
Towering 10-ft wet-meadow sunflower with many small yellow daisies on reddish stems. Stout enough not to flop. Goldfinches strip the seed heads in fall.

Earliest spring bloomer in the woods — flowers emerge from leathery overwintered foliage before new leaves. Distinguished from round-lobed hepatica by pointed (not rounded) leaf lobes. Prefers limestone soils.
Tiny grass-leaved corm-bulb with cheerful 6-petaled yellow stars over many weeks. Looks like a miniature daffodil-relative. Easy reseeder on lean dry soils.
Most drought-tolerant blazing star — large rounded flower clusters along the stem like spaced buttons. Late-season nectar for migrating monarchs.
Largest individual flower heads of any blazing star — open globes the size of golf balls. Conservation-listed in some Northeast states; less common than L. spicata in the trade.
Distinctive squared-off recurved phyllary bracts give the species name "squarrosa" (out-of-square). Earliest blazing star to bloom; shortest in stature.
Nodding yellow-to-orange bell-shaped flowers (petals less reflexed than Turk's-cap lilies). Up to 20 flowers per stem on mature plants. Wet woodland edges and meadows.
Native loosestrife (NOT the invasive purple) — downturned yellow star flowers from upper leaf axils. Fringed leaf-stalk hairs give the species name. Specialist host for oil-collecting Macropis bees.

Native Lythrum — wiry stems with magenta flowers in upper leaf axils all summer. DO NOT confuse with invasive purple loosestrife (*L. salicaria*), which has dense terminal spikes and lance leaves. Winged stems are diagnostic.
Carpet-forming two-leaved groundcover of northern woods. Small fluffy spikes of white flowers above heart-shaped leaves, ripening into speckled red berries. Tolerates dry shade once established.

Distinctive open star-shaped white flowers (not the dense fluff of M. racemosum). Arching wider-leaved stems; spreads steadily by rhizomes. Berries ripen striped-then-red. Formerly *Smilacina stellata*.
Almost identical to *M. ringens* but with winged stems and stalked (not stalkless) leaves. Snapdragon-like lavender-purple flowers in upper leaf axils. Floodplain and streambank.
Evergreen creeping vine-like groundcover. Pairs of fragrant white trumpet flowers fuse at the base into a single red berry, two flower scars visible. Berries persist through winter.
Spike of intricate snowflake-shaped tiny white flowers — only visible up close. Two opposite leaves clasp the flowering stalk (the species name). Charming connoisseur plant for shade gardens.
Day-blooming evening primrose — bright yellow cup flowers open in morning, unlike the night-blooming species. Long bloom for weeks. Reliable mid-border perennial.

Wet-prairie goldenrod with distinctive folded V-shaped leaves held nearly vertical. Flat-topped (not plumed) flower clusters. Conservation concern as wet prairies disappear. Formerly *Solidago riddellii*.
Tall white-flowered umbel of wet meadows. Resembles cultivated parsley relatives but TOXIC like its cousin water hemlock — never confuse with edible carrots. Important late-season pollinator nectar.
Compact Penstemon with pale lavender-pink tubular flowers and hairy stems/foliage. Evergreen basal rosette. Smaller and tougher than *P. digitalis* for the dry rock-garden border.
Cylindrical pyramidal pink flower clusters earlier than *P. paniculata*. Purple-spotted stems give the species name. Significantly more powdery-mildew-resistant than garden phlox.
Mid-height prairie phlox with downy soft-hairy foliage and pink-lavender flowers. Earlier and tougher than garden phlox; the drier-soil counterpart to *P. divaricata*.
Snapdragon-like flowers in four-sided spikes — each flower stays put when pushed sideways (hence 'obedient'). Aggressive rhizomatous spreader in good soil; contain in a defined bed.
Unforgettable flower — petals fully reflexed backward like a comet trailing past dark anthers. Buzz-pollinated by bumblebees. Goes summer-dormant after seed-set. Formerly *Dodecatheon meadia*.

Distinctive whitened (hoary) upper leaves act as flower-like bracts long before and after actual bloom. Spotted pale lavender flowers. Pollinator powerhouse in shade tolerance Pycnanthemum lacks elsewhere.
Top-rated pollinator plant alongside *P. tenuifolium*. Distinguished by broader leaves than slender mountain mint. Aromatic; spreads steadily by rhizomes.

Tall yellow coneflower with drooping ray petals and a tall green central cone. Deeply cut leaves. Spreads aggressively in rich moist soils — site where it can colonize freely. Parent of double-flowered heirloom 'Golden Glow'.
Burgundy-tinted basal rosette of lyre-shaped leaves persists in mild winters. Spike of pale blue-violet two-lipped flowers in spring. Self-sows freely on lean soils.
Wedges into rock crevices and ledges (the name *saxifraga* means "rock-breaker"). Basal rosette of toothed succulent leaves; airy panicle of tiny white flowers in earliest spring.

Only native shade-tolerant Sedum — all others are sun lovers. Pale green succulent leaves in whorls of three. Star-shaped white flowers in spring. Excellent groundcover for shady rock walls and slopes.
White star-shaped flowers with deeply fringed petals — each petal cut into thread-like segments. Whorled leaves and airy panicle give an unusually delicate look for a native.
Brilliant crimson-scarlet star flowers with notched petals — magnet for ruby-throated hummingbirds. Short-lived but self-sows readily on rocky woodland slopes. One of the showiest red natives.
Whorls of three leaves around square stems; loose panicles of yellow daisies for weeks. More refined than cup plant (*S. perfoliatum*) — doesn't flop in good gardens.
Shade-tolerant goldenrod with distinctive zigzagging stem and broad ovate leaves (very un-goldenrod-like). Yellow flower clusters in upper leaf axils. Spreads steadily in rich woodland soil.
Earliest goldenrod to flower — often starts in late June, weeks before showy and Canada goldenrods. Pyramidal plumes with one-sided arching branches.
Shade-tolerant goldenrod with toothed elm-like leaves and graceful arching one-sided branches. Mt. Cuba goldenrod trials ranked it a top performer in part shade.
Glossy lance-shaped leaves on smooth purplish stems. Wet-meadow aster with pale blue rays. Often lumped with swamp aster (*S. puniceum*). Formerly *Aster firmus*.

Shade-tolerant aster with arrow-shaped lower leaves on winged petioles. White to pale blue rays in airy panicles. Less common than its sister *S. cordifolium* in the trade. Formerly *Aster sagittifolius*.
Tall airy meadow rue with purple-tinged stems and fluffy white-to-pinkish flowers (no petals — just stamens). Lacy columbine-like foliage. Wind-pollinated, dioecious.
Sometimes treated as a synonym of *T. polygamum*. Tall cloud-like white-puff inflorescences over delicate columbine-like foliage. Eastern wet-meadow counterpart of *T. dasycarpum*.
Delicate spring ephemeral with white-pink anemone-like flowers above tiny columbine-like leaves. Cross between true Thalictrum and true Anemone in look. Formerly *Anemonella thalictroides*.
White nodding trillium with the flower held below or beside the leaves on a bent pedicel. Wider petals than its sibling *T. cernuum*. Slow-growing; never dig from wild populations.
Stalkless dark-maroon upright flower sitting directly on the whorl of mottled silver-and-green leaves. Faint carrion scent attracts fly pollinators. Smaller than *T. cuneatum* but similar look.
Slender stems appear pierced through the leaves (perfoliate); twisted pale-yellow bell flowers nod beneath. Quieter sister of *U. grandiflora* — smaller flowers, daintier presence.
Smallest of the bellworts — delicate pale-yellow bells on arching stems. Stalkless (sessile) leaves don't pierce the stem (vs. perfoliate bellwort). Spreads steadily by rhizomes into colonies.
Densely white-felted gray-green leaves (hoary). Stiff upright purple flower spikes for months. Excellent drought tolerance; thrives on the worst dry sites where blue vervain struggles.
Heart-shaped basal leaves distinguish it from the lacy-leaved sister *Z. aurea*. Flat-topped yellow umbels in spring. Black swallowtail host plant.
Common cool-season wet-meadow grass with arching purple-tinged flower panicles aging to straw-gold. Spreads by rhizomes into solid stands. Important nesting cover for waterbirds.
Easiest woodland sedge for gardens — tolerates dry shade, foot traffic, and lawn-style mowing. Bright lime-green semi-evergreen mounds; widely substituted for non-native lawn grass in shade.
Distinctive 1-inch-wide pleated puckered evergreen leaves — far broader than typical sedges. Reddish-purple flower spikes in spring. Popular under-tree groundcover, clump-forming.
Striking bluish-silver wide leaves — the most colorful native sedge for shade. Clump-forming; tolerates dry shade once established. Beautiful contrast against dark-green woodland ferns.
Tall arching shade-tolerant grass for floodplain and woodland edges. Open nodding flower panicles in late summer. Faint sweet hay scent when crushed.
Glossy arching dark-green leaves give a clean look in deep shade — one of the few truly shade-thriving native grasses. Spreads slowly by rhizomes. Distinctive beaked grain at maturity.
Southeastern aster with large lavender-blue fringed flowers in early summer. Tolerant of poor, sandy soils and a reliable cut flower.
Grown for its show-stopping clusters of magenta berries that ring the stems in fall and feed songbirds. Berries make a folk jelly.
Woodland gem with upright red tubular flowers flaring to a yellow star inside. A hummingbird magnet for part-shade beds.
Statuesque swamp mallow with dinner-plate scarlet flowers all summer. Thrives in wet soils and pond edges where few perennials bloom so boldly.
Tropical sage blooms scarlet spikes from late spring to frost and reseeds freely. One of the longest-blooming nectar sources for hummingbirds.
The iconic evergreen of the South: glossy leaves and huge, lemon-scented white blooms. Beetle-pollinated, with cone-like fruit whose red seeds feed wildlife.
Fall-blooming tickseed of wet pine savannas, carrying yellow daisies into November when most natives have finished. Good for damp, acidic spots.
The milkweed for wet ground: white-to-blush umbels over a long season and a monarch host plant where drier milkweeds won't grow. Often reblooms.
